Curriculum → Networking, edge, and request routing → Rate limiting and edge protection

Lesson 5 of 5

Rate limiting and edge protection

Protect critical services from abusive clients, noisy neighbors, and self-inflicted overload before the core system becomes unstable.

25-35 min Networking, edge, and request routing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Abuse resistance is part of system design; a product that collapses under spikes or bots is not production ready.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Choose the right limiting unit Token bucket is common for burst allowance2. Place enforcement carefully Use edge limits for broad abuse patterns3. Plan the user experience Return structured retry headers or error bodies

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Abuse resistance is part of system design; a product that collapses under spikes or bots is not production ready. 2. Choose the right limiting unit Token bucket is common for burst allowance3. Place enforcement carefully Use edge limits for broad abuse patterns4. Plan the user experience Return structured retry headers or error bodies
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Abuse resistance is part of system design; a product that collapses under spikes or bots is not production ready.

What to say first

  • Name who is being limited and why.
  • Choose an algorithm that matches burst tolerance.
  • Place coarse protection at the edge and fine limits near expensive resources.
2

Study section

Choose the right limiting unit

Limits can apply per user, API key, IP, tenant, region, or endpoint depending on product semantics and attack surface.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Token bucket is common for burst allowance
  • Leaky bucket smooths output rate
  • Sliding window logs are accurate but more expensive
3

Study section

Place enforcement carefully

Rate limits near the edge protect origin resources early, while service-local limits protect expensive internals with finer semantics.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use edge limits for broad abuse patterns
  • Use per-tenant quotas deeper in the stack
  • Combine limits with queue back pressure and circuit breakers
4

Study section

Plan the user experience

Good systems return clear retry semantics, degrade gracefully, and distinguish between accidental bursts and malicious behavior.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Return structured retry headers or error bodies
  • Prefer soft limits plus warnings for internal customers
  • Track block rates and false positives

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Name who is being limited and why.
  • Choose an algorithm that matches burst tolerance.
  • Place coarse protection at the edge and fine limits near expensive resources.
  • Explain retry messaging and observability.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Using IP-only limits for authenticated multi-tenant workloads.
  • Relying on a single centralized limiter that becomes a bottleneck.
  • Blocking without clear client feedback or monitoring.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you protect a login API from credential stuffing?
  • When would you rate-limit by tenant instead of by user?
  • What metrics tell you the limiter is too strict or too weak?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Latency, throughput, and SLOs Foundations and estimationIdempotency, retries, and back pressure Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Rate limiting and edge protection

Watch the edge, cache, and origin layers react as traffic shape changes across regions and protection controls. Apply it to rate limiting and edge protection so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Rate limiting and edge protection" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around choose the right limiting unit and watch how it interacts with place enforcement carefully.Abuse resistance is part of system design; a product that collapses under spikes or bots is not production ready.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • CDN hit ratio
  • origin utilization
  • edge p95
  • policy saturation

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 6: service · 50000 rps · 6 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • Name who is being limited and why.
  • Choose an algorithm that matches burst tolerance.
  • Place coarse protection at the edge and fine limits near expensive resources.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Choose the right limiting unit and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like Name who is being limited and why. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Place enforcement carefully.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Rate limiting and edge protection" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • How would you protect a login API from credential stuffing?
  • Choose the right limiting unit
  • Place enforcement carefully
  • Name who is being limited and why.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
